pages.text: Monitoring the strength development in concrete by EMI sensing technique R. Tawie, H.K. Lee * Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, 373-1 Guseong-dong, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-701, South Korea article info Article history: Received 6 October 2009 Received in revised form 19 January 2010 Accepted 2 February 2010 Available online 1 March 2010 Keywords: EMI sensing Nondestructive evaluation PZT patches Strength development Statistics metrics abstract For any new nondestructive evaluation (NDE) technique, prior to full commercialization and real ﬁeld application, rigorous studies are needed to address its practicality and reproducibility of the results under nominally the same conditions. Recently, the applicability of electro-mechanical impedance (EMI) sens- ing technique has been extended to monitoring of concrete curing and strength gain. Experimental inves- tigations of this relatively new NDE technique for damage detection in structures have been successfully demonstrated by many researchers. The objective of the present study is to further develop the applica- tion of the EMI sensing technique for in situ monitoring of concrete using the low cost piezoceramic (PZT) patches. In this investigation, resonant frequencies of the surface bonded PZT patches were tracked using an impedance analyzer by sweeping the frequency within the range of 100–400 kHz. The experimental results verify the practicality of the EMI sensing technique for monitoring the strength development in concrete. Using an established empirical relationship, the relative strength gain of concrete can be pre- dicted by monitoring the resonant frequency shift. The quantitative approach based on the statistics met- rics such as RMSD, MAPD and CCD to compare the EMI spectra is also presented. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Until today, more concrete have been used in buildings and civil infrastructures than any other materials. Concrete, which is made from heterogeneous mixture of cement, aggregates, water and admixtures, is more popular due to its comparatively low cost and its versatility for meeting the performance requirements of different types of structures. In practice, structural members made of concrete can be cast in situ or pre-cast in a factory and installed at the construction site. Pre-cast concrete products exhibit high quality and uniformity than in situ concrete due to high standard of production in factory conditions. Quality of in situ concrete var- ies depending on quality of materials supplied to the site, construc- tion techniques, workmanship and exposures [1]. Therefore, monitoring of quality of in situ concrete is very important to en- sure that the structures built using concrete are structurally ade- quate for the purpose which it was designed. Compressive strength is one of the most important parameters used for quality control of concrete. By testing on cube or cylinder specimens, the compressive strength of concrete is determined at a standard age, usually 28 days since the bulk of its ultimate strength is expected to develop by that period [2]. Strength of concrete shall continue to increase at a diminishing rate thereafter and it may take years before concrete reaches its ultimate strength. It is important to monitor strength of concrete so that factors inﬂuenc- ing the relative strength gain of concrete over time after mixing can be identiﬁed. Theref

of a probe into concrete as a measure of the penetration resistance of the material. Within certain limits of material composition and testing conditions, good correlation can be found with in situ strength of concrete using these methods [6]. Advances in piezoelectric materials, have attracted interests among researchers to develop new nondestructive evaluation (NDE) techniques. One such technique that has made much pro- gress is the electro-mechanical impedance (EMI) sensing tech- nique, which employs piezo-impedance transducers. The transducers exhibit piezoelectric effects which means they can produce a voltage difference across its top and bottom faces when compressed (direct effect), and conversely, they can physically change shape when an external electric ﬁeld is applied (converse effect). EMI sensing technique utilizes high frequencies in the range of 30–400 kHz to electrically excite the piezo-impedance transducers bonded on a host structure by means of alternating electrical ﬁeld source to indirectly induce dynamic response in the structure [7]. The piezo transducers, which act as a collocated actuator and sensor, can capture this response in the form of elec- trical admittance or impedance (inverse of admittance) that serves as function of the interaction between the mechanical and electri- cal impedance of the structure and the transducers, respectively. A change in the measured electrical admittance or impedance spec- trum indicates a change in the structural conditions. Detail expla- nations of the EMI sensing technique can be found in Liang et al. [8] and Giurgiutu and Rogers [9]. Application of the EMI sensing technique to concrete structures began relatively late than other ﬁelds. Experimental implementa- tion for damage detection in concrete structures has been success- fully demonstrated by Soh et al. [10], Park et al. [11], Bhalla and Soh [12] and Tseng and Wang [13]. Since then, constant researches in the ﬁelds related to structural health monitoring (SHM) of civil infrastructures have been carried out by various researchers. The proof-of-concept of the EMI sensing technique for predicting con- crete strength was ﬁrst investigated by Soh and Bhalla [14]. They showed that a strong correlation exists between cube compressive strength of concrete and resonant frequency of the EMI spectrum. The feasibility of the EMI sensing technique was further investi- gated by Shin et al. [15,16] using a cylindrical specimen and a dif- ferent PZT installation technique. It was found that the root mean square deviation (RMSD) index, which is a common index used in damage detection in structures, can be a suitable and sensitive indicator for the strength gain monitoring of concrete. Findings from these studies show that this method may not yet be ready for commercialization until issues regarding its practicality and reliability of measurements are addressed through more investiga- tions. In the present study, the objective was to further investigate the applicability of the EMI sensing technique for in situ monitor- ing of concrete using the low cost piezoceramic (PZT) patches. The effects of main parameters in the mix proportion of concrete such as water–cement ratio and cement content, which inﬂuence the relative strength gain of concrete, on the measured EMI resonance spectra were investigated. The sensitivity of the RMSD metric as a reliable indicator for monitoring strength development in concrete was also compared with the mea

impedance analyzer, for example 100–400 kHz as used in this study, a change in the EMI spectra implies an interaction between the PZT and the host structure reﬂecting a change in characteristics of the structure. Therefore, the shifting of the resonance spectra of the bonded PZT with time in this study is on account of the hard- ening of concrete during curing process. This becomes the basis of the EMI sensing technique for monitoring strength gain of concrete. The HP 4294A impedance analyzer enables EMI spectra to be displayed graphically during measurements. This permits immedi- ate analysis of the resonant frequencies and admittance or imped- ance values. The resonant frequencies of the EMI spectra can be easily located using the marker function. Fig. 4 shows the effect of curing age on the average resonant frequency of the bonded PZT patches for all the mixes. It can be seen that resonant frequen- cies of the EMI spectra increase with curing age. This shows that the PZT patches are capable of monitoring changes occurred during curing process of concrete. For evaluating the strength gain of con- crete, the average resonant frequency of day 3 was used as refer- ence and the resonant frequency shift at days 7, 14 and 28, respectively, was calculated cumulatively (see Fig. 5 ). For all the mixes, a large amount of shift was observed between day 3 and day 7 compared to between day 7 and day 14 and between day 14 and day 28. This agrees with the fact that concrete gains strength most rapidly in the early ages up to day 7 as shown in Fig. 6 , and then the strength gain becomes much lesser after that. In the mix proportion, water and cement contents are the two most important variables that can inﬂuence the strength develop- ment in concrete. The w/c ratio for mixes A, B, C and D was 0.44, 0.50, 0.57 and 0.67, respectively. The increase in w/c ratio in this study corresponded to a decrease in cement content from 450 to 300 kg/m 3 for a given water content of 200 kg/m 3. As can be seen in Fig. 6 , as w/c ratio increases, the compressive strength of con- crete at 3, 7, 14 and 28 days of curing decreases. The relationship between the average resonant frequency shift of bonded PZT patches at day 28 and strength gain of concrete from day 3 to 28 for all the mixes is shown in Fig. 7. Also included is the coefﬁcient of determination (r 2), which is a measure of the strength of the linear relationship between the two variables. As can be seen, a strong correlation exists between resonant frequency shift of the EMI spectra of bonded PZT patches and strength gain of con- crete. This shows that the resonant frequency shift is a good indi- cator for strength gain monitoring of concrete. In Shin et al. played as a function of frequency. Only the real component of the admittance which is the main data used in structural health mon- itoring was analyzed. The imaginary component, which is more sensitive to temperature variation and can be used to diagnose the integrity of the bonded PZT patches [18], were not considered in this study assuming that the quality of PZT patches and bonding conditions do not degrade during the monitoring period. According to Park et al. [18], a change in the slope of the imaginary compo- nent of the electrical admittance implies a change in the capacitive value of the PZT. However, in this study, the functionality of the PZT patches was checked using an Agilent digital multimeter by measuring the capacitance of the bonded PZT patches. Only PZT B2 showed no reading when tested on the third day of curing of the concrete specimens indicating a problem with the device. The measured capacitive values of the bonded PZT patches were found to be in the range of 2.9–3.3 nF. The difference can be attrib- uted to the variations in the PZT patches and their bonding conditions. Fig. 2 shows the spectrum of the real component of the electri- cal admittance Re( Y) for the free PZT. As can be seen, the resonant frequencies of the free PZT were located at about 170, 240, 450 and 780 kHz. It is noted that the selected sweeping frequency range of 100–400 kHz used in the EMI measurements was chosen because beyond 500 kHz the change in the EMI spectrum will be inﬂuenced by the condition of the bonded PZT rather than the condition of the host structure [7]. In Fig. 3 a–g, the EMI spectra for the bonded PZT patches ob- tained for specimens of mixes A, B, C and D at days 3, 7, 14 and 28 are presented, respectively. As already explained, the EMI spec- tra of PZT B2 were not available due to failure of the PZT. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a–g that the EMI resonance spectra shift to the right direction as the curing age increases. It is encouraging to see the changes in the EMI spectra correspond with the curing age. Immediately after placement, concrete starts to harden and gains strength with time. The hardening process caused the stiffen- ing of the bonded PZT and in turn caused the shifting of the EMI spectra. The horizontal shifts of the EMI resonance spectra were also observed in the study by Shin et al. [15,16]. However, in their study, the resonance peak shape can only be seen clearly in one case (air cured specimen), while the monitoring results of their uncontrolled curing case were distorted due to tight initial bonding of the PZT patches. In the case of the air cured condition which was the same as in our study, as the curing age increased, the ﬁrst res- onant peaks subsided and at the same time the second resonance peaks appeared strongly. In our study, the average magnitude of the resonance peaks decreased with time and most signiﬁcantly from day 3 to 7 as shown in Fig. 3a–g. The second peaks were only noticeable on day 3 and were completely suppressed after 28 days of curing. Soh and Bhalla [14], who were the ﬁrst to conduct the monitoring of concrete curing using PZT patches, observed that the peak not only shifted to the right direction but also getting sharper with time. They attributed the sharp peak as a result of reduction of damping in concrete as the moisture content dropped. From our results and the previous studies, it is evident that the cur- ing condition is one of the signiﬁcant factors that caused the change

from day 3 to 28 was 54 and 53 kHz for the water bath and air cured specimens, respectively, while the average strength gain (day 3–28) of the two specimens was 15 and 14 MPa, respectively. Based on their ﬁndings, the effect of curing condition on the reso- nant frequency shift and strength gain of concrete was not so sig- niﬁcant. However, it is important to note that the PZT patches used in the EMI measurements in our (t = 0.5 mm) and their study (t = 0.2 mm) was of different thickness. This shows that the vibra- tion characteristics of a bonded PZT patch can be affected by its thickness. Hence, the use of the same type and size of the PZT patches for monitoring is recommended unless proper calibration is done. 3.3. Evaluation using statistics metrics In the previous subsection, only frequency shifts were used to evaluate the effect of curing of concrete although both frequency shifts and changes in amplitude were observed in the EMI spectra. To account for the overall change in the EMI spectra during the cur- ing process, they can be processed using statistics metrics such as root mean square deviation (RMSD), mean absolute percentage deviation (MAPD) and correlation coefﬁcient deviation (CCD), which are commonly used in structural health monitoring of struc- tures as damage indices [19]. In this paper, the mathematical expressions of these metrics in terms of the real component of the electrical admittance Re( Y) of bonded PZT, are given as follows [19]: RMSD ð%Þ¼ XN k¼1 ReðYkÞj /C0 ReðYkÞi hi 2 XN k¼1 ReðYkÞi /C2/C3 2 , ! 1=2 ð1Þ MAPD ð%Þ¼ 1 N XN k¼1 j½ReðYkÞj /C0 ReðYkÞi/C138 =ReðYkÞijð 2Þ CCD ð%Þ¼ 1 NrYj rYi XN k¼1 ReðYkÞj /C0 Reð/C22YÞj hi /C1 ReðYkÞi /C0 Reð/C22YÞi /C2/C3 ð3Þ where N is the number of sample points in the EMI spectra (N = 801), the subscripts i and j signify the two compared curing age and the symbols /C22Y and rY signify the mean value and standard deviation, respectively. Eqs. (1)–(3) yield a numerical value, which quantiﬁes the differ- ence between two EMI spectra of different curing age. With the use of these equations, any frequency shifts and changes in amplitude in the EMI spectra are accounted for. The larger the difference be- tween the two compared EMI spectra the greater the numerical values of the metrics. In this study, the numerical values are inter- preted as an indicator for the amount of strength gain of concrete during curing process. The calculated values of the statistics met- rics for mixes A, B, C and D, respectively, are shown in Fig. 8a–d . Among the three metrics, RMSD and CCD is the most and the least sensitive, respectively. Greater change is observed between EMI spectra of day 3 ( ith curing age) and day 7 ( jth curing age) for all the metrics. This is expected since about 30% of 28-day compres- sive strength of the concrete was achieved between day 3 and 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 80 RMSD MAPD CCD (a) Mix A 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 80 7_3 14_7 28_14 7_3 14_7 28_14 RMSD MAPD CCD (b) Mix B Compared curing days % Compared curing days % 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 RMSD MAPD CCD (c) Mix C 0 10 20 30 40 50 7_3 14_7 28_14 7_3 14_7 28_14 RMSD MAPD CCD (d) Mix D Compared curing days % Compared curing days % Fig. 8. Calculated statistics metrics for all the mixes. R. Tawie, H.K. Lee / Construction and Building Materials 24 (2010) 1746–1753 1751

day 7 for all the mixes and less than 20% was gained either be- tween day 7 and day 14 or between day 14 and day 28 (see Fig. 6 ). By cumulatively adding up the RMSD, MAPD and CCD val- ues at days 7, 14, and 28, the increase in the metrics values is quite similar to the trend in strength development in concrete. From a comparison with the strength gain from day 3 to 28 for all the mixes, MAPD was found to be better correlated than RMSD and CCD. Fig. 9 shows that the value of MAPD metric seems to in- crease linearly with the strength gain, which suggests that it could also be a reliable indicator. Compared to the resonant frequency approach, the statistic metrics are not inﬂuenced by the variations due to operation conditions [19]. It was shown in Shin et al. [15] that tight initial bonding of the PZT can distort the EMI spectrum and the resonant frequency cannot be easily identiﬁed. In this case, the metric approach can be very useful to interpret and quantify the information from different data sets. However, Soh and Bhalla [14] have shown that the resonant frequencies shift to the left when concrete is subjected to stress. The disadvantage of the met- ric approach is that the shifting direction of the EMI spectra cannot be known if based upon the numerical values of the metrics be- cause only positive values are produced. The shifting direction can only be noted from the EMI spectra and therefore the metric values should be interpreted together with the EMI spectra plots. 3.4. Empirical curve ﬁt for monitoring strength development in concrete The results in this investigation clearly indicate that the strength gain of concrete can either be monitored using the reso- nant frequency shift or numerical values calculated using statistics metrics. Tracking the resonant frequency directly using an imped- ance analyzer does present practical advantages, while data ob- tained from the EMI measurements need to be processed to calculate the metric values. As it was shown that the resonant fre- quency shift has a better correlation with strength gain of concrete, this provides for higher accuracy for strength prediction. Conse- quently, in this study, only resonant frequency shift results were correlated with relative strength–age data (S /S u) determined from standard testing procedure to provide for strength gain prediction. Regression analysis showed that the relative strength gain of con- crete predicted using the experimental data is best ﬁt with expo- nential function in the form of: S Su ¼ c1 /C1 expðc2AÞð 4Þ where A is the resonant frequency shift measured in kHz and c1 and c2 are the empirical coefﬁcients. The empirical coefﬁcients for all the mixes are given in Table 3 and the empirical curve ﬁt for mon- itoring the strength development in concrete based on the average resonant frequency shift of bonded PZT are presented in Fig. 10 .I n Table 3, value of c 2 increases as the w/c ratio increases from 0.44 to 0.67, while coefﬁcient c1 becomes more pronounced with a de- crease in w/c ratio. The effect of w/c ratio as seen in Fig. 10 shows that concrete with lower w/c ratio developed greater early strength and ultimate strength but slower rate of strength gain than those with higher w/c ratio. Besides w/c ratio, the rate of strength gain also depends on type of cement [20]. It should be noted the empir- ical curve ﬁt as shown in Fig. 10 is expected to be inﬂuenced by cur- ing methods and temperature which was not considered in this study.

CCD from a comparison with the strength gain from day 3 to 28 for all the mixes. In order to implement the NDE technique for in situ monitoring of concrete, an empirical relationship needs to be established to predict the relative strength gain of concrete. It is found that the strength gain of concrete for all the mixes showed an increasing exponential trend with increasing resonant fre- quency shift. Further studies are required to investigate the effects of ambient temperature and relative humidity on the EMI spectra.
